learningExperiences.title: Architectural Audit, Blueprint, Business Communication & Proposal Evaluation

contentBlocks.content:
WHY THIS MATTERS

Every preceding section built a specific competency. This section is where those competencies become a consulting skill. An architect who can reason about fiber scheduling, design a state topology, and select a library stack still needs to do three things that matter to a client: audit what exists, prescribe what should exist, and communicate why — in terms the client acts on. This section formalises those three skills.

LEARNING OBJECTIVES

By the end of this section, you will be able to:
1. Conduct a structured architectural audit using six lenses and produce a prioritised liability list.
2. Design a complete architectural blueprint from a product requirements brief.
3. Justify architectural decisions in business trade-off terms for non-technical stakeholders.
4. Evaluate two competing proposals and produce a written recommendation with explicit, defensible criteria and a review trigger.

KEY TERMS & DEFINITIONS

Architectural audit — A structured review of an existing codebase identifying architectural liabilities: patterns that increase maintenance cost, introduce bugs, or block future feature development.

Architectural liability — A specific, demonstrable problem with a measurable impact: a bug class it creates, a performance cost, a feature it blocks. Not a style opinion.

Architectural blueprint — A pre-implementation design document covering state topology, component model, async boundary placement, library selections, and testing strategy.

Remediation priority — The ranking of liabilities by: severity of impact × cost to fix × risk of not fixing. High: user-facing bugs or blocked features. Medium: compounding maintenance cost. Low: best practice violation with no current measurable impact.

Business trade-off — An architectural decision expressed in terms a non-technical stakeholder can act on: time-to-market, maintenance cost, team scalability, reliability — not implementation details.

Technical debt — Architectural decisions expedient at the time that accumulate interest: each subsequent change costs more because of them.

Review trigger — An explicit condition that would invalidate the current architectural decision and require revisiting it. Prevents decisions from hardening into unchallenged assumptions.

PART 1: THE STRUCTURED CODEBASE AUDIT

An audit without structure produces an opinion. A structured audit produces a prioritised remediation plan.

THE SIX AUDIT LENSES:

Lens 1: Rendering model (Section 1)
- Are there unnecessary re-render cascades?
- Is memo/useMemo/useCallback applied speculatively or evidently?
- Are there side effects in render functions?

Lens 2: Async boundary design (Section 2)
- Are Suspense and Error Boundaries used, and at what granularity?
- Are loading/error states scattered as inline booleans instead?
- Are there waterfall data fetches where parallel would be correct?

Lens 3: TypeScript contract quality (Section 3)
- Is any used? Where?
- Are state shapes modelled as discriminated unions or as flag combinations?
- Are domain IDs distinguishable at the type level?

Lens 4: State topology (Section 5)
- Is server state stored in a global client store?
- Is Context used as a state manager rather than a DI mechanism?
- Is there prop drilling indicating misplaced state ownership?
- Is derived state stored and manually synchronised?

Lens 5: Component design (Section 6)
- Are components monolithic, mixing data fetching, business logic, and rendering?
- Are there headless hook opportunities where behavior and presentation are coupled?
- Are Portals used correctly for modals and overlays?

Lens 6: Performance and testing (Sections 9 & 10)
- Are heavy dependencies code-split?
- Does the test suite verify behavior or implementation details?
- Is there bundle analysis in the CI pipeline?

LIABILITY DOCUMENTATION FORMAT:
  LIABILITY: [name]
  Lens: [which of the six]
  Evidence: [specific file/component/pattern]
  Impact: [what it causes — bugs, performance, slow development]
  Remediation: [specific change required]
  Priority: High / Medium / Low
  Effort: [rough estimate — hours / days / sprint]

PRIORITISATION:
- High: causes user-facing bugs or performance issues; blocks new feature development.
- Medium: increases maintenance cost; creates compounding technical debt.
- Low: violates best practice but has no current measurable impact.
Fix High first, always. Medium in planned sprints. Low only if incidental to other work.

PART 2: THE ARCHITECTURAL BLUEPRINT

When starting a new feature or greenfield project, produce a blueprint before writing code. A blueprint takes 2-4 hours and prevents weeks of rework.

BLUEPRINT SECTIONS (in this order — state topology ALWAYS first):

1. STATE TOPOLOGY MAP
List every state the feature requires. Classify and assign ownership.
  State             | Kind          | Owner / Mechanism
  ------------------|---------------|---------------------------
  Subscription data | Server cache  | TanStack Query
  Current user      | Global client | Zustand
  Plan limits       | Derived       | useMemo from subscription
  Upgrade modal     | Ephemeral     | useState (local)
  Upgrade form      | Form          | React Hook Form + Zod

2. ASYNC BOUNDARY MAP
Draw the Suspense and Error Boundary placement.
  <FeatureErrorBoundary fallback={<BillingError />}>
    <Suspense fallback={<UsageSkeleton />}>
      <UsagePanel />              ← usage + plan data
    </Suspense>
    <FeatureErrorBoundary fallback={<InvoiceError />}>
      <Suspense fallback={<InvoiceSkeleton />}>
        <InvoiceHistory />        ← independent region
      </Suspense>
    </FeatureErrorBoundary>
  </FeatureErrorBoundary>
  {upgradeModalOpen && (
    <Portal><UpgradeModal onClose={...} /></Portal>  ← escapes stacking context
  )}

3. COMPONENT MODEL
Identify presentational vs. container components, which patterns apply (compound? headless? portal?), and where behavior should be extracted to hooks.

4. TYPESCRIPT CONTRACT DECISIONS
List any discriminated unions (async state shapes), branded types (domain IDs), and generic hooks needed. Example:
  type PlanId = string & { __brand: 'PlanId' };
  type InvoiceId = string & { __brand: 'InvoiceId' };
  const upgradeSchema = z.object({ planId: z.string().min(1), billingPeriod: z.enum(['monthly', 'annual']) });
  type UpgradeFormData = z.infer<typeof upgradeSchema>;

5. LIBRARY SELECTIONS
Explicit per-feature decisions with justification tied to the feature's state characteristics — not general preferences.

6. TESTING STRATEGY
Which integration tests cover the happy path? Which cover the critical error path? Which pure functions warrant unit tests?

PART 3: COMMUNICATING ARCHITECTURE TO NON-TECHNICAL STAKEHOLDERS

The most technically correct architectural decision fails if the client can't evaluate it. SMB SaaS clients make staffing, timeline, and scope decisions that architectural choices directly constrain.

THE TRANSLATION PRINCIPLE: Every architectural decision has a business consequence. Express it in that language.

  Architectural decision         | Business translation
  -------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------
  TanStack Query vs. Redux       | 'Users always see fresh data automatically — no manual refresh needed. Reduces the stale data support ticket class by ~60%.'
  Route-level code splitting     | 'The app loads 40% faster on first visit. Dashboard-only users never download reports code.'
  Zod as single source of truth  | 'Validation rules defined once, enforced everywhere. Eliminates the most common class of form-accepts/API-rejects bugs.'
  RTL + MSW testing              | 'Tests verify what users experience, not internal wiring. Tests survive refactoring — we can improve code without breaking the safety net.'

WHAT NOT TO SAY: 'We're using a discriminated union state model because it eliminates impossible states at the type level.' Technically precise. Communicatively useless.

THE THREE-PART BUSINESS FRAMING:
1. What decision was made (one sentence, no jargon).
2. What risk or cost it prevents (specific, quantifiable if possible).
3. What the alternative would have cost (makes the trade-off visible).

PART 4: EVALUATING COMPETING PROPOSALS

SMB SaaS clients frequently present competing architectural approaches. Producing a defensible recommendation requires explicit criteria — not instinct.

THE EVALUATION FRAMEWORK:
  Criterion                   | Weight | Proposal A | Proposal B
  ----------------------------|--------|------------|------------
  Correctness for this domain | High   |            |
  Team maintainability        | High   |            |
  Time-to-first-delivery      | Med    |            |
  Long-term extensibility     | Med    |            |
  Bundle size / performance   | Low    |            |
  Reversibility if wrong      | Med    |            |

Weights are context-dependent. For an SMB SaaS with a 4-person team and 18-month runway: maintainability outweighs sophistication. For a series-B product with a 20-person eng org: extensibility and team scalability increase in weight.

PRODUCING THE RECOMMENDATION:
1. State the winning proposal and the primary criterion it wins on.
2. Acknowledge what it trades away.
3. State the condition under which the losing proposal would be correct.
4. Recommend a review trigger: 'If X happens, we revisit this decision.'

Example: 'Proposal A (Zustand + TanStack Query) is recommended. It wins on team maintainability and time-to-delivery for a team of four with no existing RTK experience. It trades away RTK's middleware pipeline and time-travel debugging — neither of which is required by the current feature set. If the product adds an audit log requirement or an event-sourced data model, RTK becomes the correct choice and migration is achievable in 1-2 sprints.'

COMMON ERRORS

Error 1: Auditing style, not architecture
'This component is too long' is a style opinion, not an architectural liability. An architectural liability has a measurable impact: a bug class, a performance cost, a feature it blocks. If you can't state the impact, it's not an audit finding.

Error 2: Prescribing the most sophisticated architecture regardless of context
RTK + Sagas + normalized entity adapters is defensible for a 50-person org with complex event-sourcing requirements. For a 4-person SMB SaaS team, it's a carrying cost with no payoff. The best architecture is the one the team can maintain given their constraints.

Error 3: Blueprint without a state topology
Starting a blueprint with component design before resolving state ownership repeats the most common React architectural mistake: designing a component hierarchy and discovering it doesn't support the required data flow. State topology always comes first.

Error 4: Communicating architecture in implementation terms
'We're using proxy-based immutability with structural sharing' communicates nothing to a product owner. 'This means we can safely undo and redo changes, and updating large data sets costs roughly 10× less than before' is actionable.

Error 5: No review trigger in architectural recommendations
Every architectural decision is correct under some set of assumptions. State the assumptions explicitly and define the condition that invalidates them. This prevents decisions from hardening into unchallenged religion as the product evolves.

interactionSequence.prompt:
Before reading: a developer presents this audit finding: 'This component is 250 lines long.' Is this an architectural liability?

A) Yes — component length is a reliable proxy for architectural problems.
B) No — component length is a style metric, not an architectural liability. A liability requires a specific measurable impact: a bug class, a performance cost, or a blocked feature.
C) Yes, but only if it exceeds 300 lines.
D) It depends on whether the component has tests.

feedback.elaborativeFeedback: B is correct. Component length is a code style observation. A 250-line component that is purely presentational, well-typed, and fully tested may have zero architectural problems. An architectural liability must have a demonstrable impact: 'This 250-line component mixes data fetching, business logic, and rendering — which means it cannot be tested without mocking the API, tested logic cannot be reused, and a data model change requires touching the rendering code.' That is a liability — the impact is testability, reusability, and coupling. 'It's 250 lines' alone is not. Every audit finding must pass the question: 'What does this cause?' If the answer is 'it looks messy,' it's not an audit finding.
feedback.correctiveFeedback: B: component length is a style metric. An architectural liability requires a specific, measurable impact — a bug class, a performance cost, a blocked feature. 'Too long' is not an impact.
hints: Could a 250-line component be architecturally excellent?
What question must every audit finding answer before it qualifies as a liability?

confidencePrompt.prompt: How confident are you in your answer? (1 = guessing, 5 = certain)

interactionSequence.prompt:
During an audit, you find useEffect used to keep a filteredUsers state variable in sync with users and filterQuery state. What category of architectural liability is this?

A) Async boundary design — useEffect is the wrong mechanism for data fetching.
B) State topology — filteredUsers is derived state being stored and manually synchronised, creating a synchronisation bug risk.
C) Component design — the filtering logic should be in a custom hook.
D) TypeScript contracts — the types are not correctly constraining the filter output.

feedback.elaborativeFeedback:
B is correct. filteredUsers is computed from users and filterQuery — it is derived state. Storing it as separate useState and synchronising via useEffect creates a class of bugs: the source state (users or filterQuery) can change between the render that queues the useEffect and the render where the effect fires, causing a render with inconsistent state. The correct fix is useMemo — compute filteredUsers inline, never store it. This is a state topology liability because the root cause is a misclassification: derived state treated as stored state.

Option C (custom hook) is not wrong as a direction, but extracting filtering to a hook doesn't fix the synchronisation problem — it just moves the useEffect into the hook. The architectural fix is removing the stored copy, not relocating it.
feedback.correctiveFeedback: B: state topology — derived state stored and manually synchronised via useEffect. The bug: source state can change between queuing and firing the effect. Fix: useMemo, never useState for derived state.
hints: Is filteredUsers stored independently from the state it's computed from?
What happens if users updates twice before the useEffect fires?

confidencePrompt.prompt: How confident are you in this answer?

interactionSequence.prompt: A product owner asks: 'Why can't we just use Redux like we always have for this new feature? We know how to use it.' Write a 3-sentence business-language response. Rules: no technical jargon (no 'staleness,' 'invalidation,' 'server cache,' 'query key,' 'staleTime'). Each sentence maps to one part of the business framing framework.

feedback.elaborativeFeedback:
Model answer applying the three-part business framing:

1. What decision was made: 'For this feature, we're using a tool designed specifically for data that comes from the server, rather than storing that data in Redux the way we have been.'

2. What risk or cost it prevents: 'With the current approach, users can see data that's hours old without knowing it — which we've seen cause support tickets when two team members see different numbers from the same screen.'

3. What the alternative would have cost: 'Continuing with Redux would mean writing and maintaining roughly 200 lines of custom logic just to keep the data fresh — code that has caused two of our last five production bugs and that this tool eliminates entirely.'

The three sentences stay in business language: the problem is stale data causing support tickets, the cost of the alternative is maintenance burden and bug frequency. No technical terms required to make the case.
feedback.correctiveFeedback: Three-part framing: (1) plain-language description of the decision, (2) what risk it prevents with a specific consequence, (3) what the alternative would have cost in concrete terms. No technical jargon in any sentence.
hints: What does the product owner care about — implementation or outcomes?
Can you quantify the cost of the current approach in lines of code or bug frequency?

confidencePrompt.prompt: How well did you apply all three parts of the business framing without jargon?

interactionSequence.prompt:
Evaluate these two proposals for state management on a new notification center (simple queue: add, dismiss, mark-read; no middleware; no audit log; 5-person team; 6-week timeline):

Proposal A: Zustand — one create() call, direct state updates.
Proposal B: Redux Toolkit — createSlice, createAsyncThunk, createSelector.

Using the evaluation framework: which wins, on which primary criterion, what does it trade away, and what is the review trigger?

feedback.elaborativeFeedback:
Proposal A (Zustand) wins.

Primary criterion: team maintainability. The notification center has simple state — a queue with three operations. None of RTK's distinguishing capabilities are required: no middleware, no time-travel debugging, no audit log, no complex entity relationships, no event sourcing. RTK's slice + actions + thunks + selectors pattern adds cognitive overhead that isn't paid back by any RTK-specific capability for this feature.

What it trades away: RTK's middleware pipeline, time-travel debugging, and strict action discipline. For this feature, none of these are needed.

Review trigger: 'If the notification center later needs to emit analytics events on each action, or the team grows and needs stricter action conventions to prevent inconsistent state updates, revisit this decision. Migrating from Zustand to RTK for a notification store is a contained, 1-day effort at that point.'

Weight calibration: for a 5-person team on a 6-week timeline, time-to-delivery and maintainability have High weight; long-term extensibility has Medium weight. Zustand wins on the two highest-weight criteria.
feedback.correctiveFeedback: Zustand wins on team maintainability — none of RTK's distinguishing features are required. Trade-off: no middleware/time-travel/action discipline. Review trigger: when analytics events or strict action conventions become requirements.
hints: Does the notification center need time-travel debugging?
Which evaluation criteria have the highest weight for a 5-person team with a 6-week timeline?

confidencePrompt.prompt: How precisely did you apply all four parts of the recommendation structure?

interactionSequence.prompt: SYNTHESIS — integrating all prior competencies through the ig-synthesis-all interleaving group: For each of the six audit lenses, name the primary section that taught it, one concrete signal that would trigger a finding under that lens, and one example liability that is often mistaken for a different lens. This exercise maps the full course to the audit framework.

feedback.elaborativeFeedback:
Lens 1 — Rendering model (Section 1): Signal: React DevTools shows a component re-rendering 50×/second with 0 state changes. Example misassignment: 'Context causes re-renders' — this is actually a State topology finding (Context used as state manager instead of DI mechanism).

Lens 2 — Async boundary design (Section 2): Signal: a single root-level Error Boundary means any feature crash goes full-page blank. Example misassignment: 'Loading state is cluttered' — this is an Async boundary finding, not a Component design finding.

Lens 3 — TypeScript contract quality (Section 3): Signal: any used in API response type, propagating to 15 downstream consumers. Example misassignment: 'Hook doesn't use discriminated union return' — this is a TypeScript contract finding, not a Component design finding.

Lens 4 — State topology (Section 5): Signal: a Redux slice stores paginated API results with manual loading/error flags that drift. Example misassignment: 'Too many useState calls' — this is often a State topology finding (over-lifting state or missing Context colocation), not a Component design finding.

Lens 5 — Component design (Section 6): Signal: a 200-line component that renders, fetches, validates, and has 4 ARIA errors. Example misassignment: 'Test is brittle' — this is a Testing finding, not Component design — unless the brittleness is caused by implementation coupling that headless extraction would fix.

Lens 6 — Performance and testing (Sections 9 & 10): Signal: 1.6MB initial bundle with no code splitting; test suite is 90% snapshots with 0 behavioral tests. Example misassignment: 'useMemo everywhere' — this is a Rendering model finding (speculative memoization), not a Performance finding.
feedback.correctiveFeedback: Each lens maps to a section: S1 → rendering, S2 → async boundaries, S3 → TS contracts, S5 → state topology, S6 → component design, S9/S10 → performance/testing. The most common cross-lens confusion: Context re-renders (state topology, not rendering), and brittle tests (testing, not component design).
hints: Which section covered each lens?
What is the most common mistake in assigning a finding to the wrong lens?

confidencePrompt.prompt: How completely did you map all six lenses to their source sections and identify the cross-lens confusions?

workedExamples.problem: A billing module written 18 months ago has: billing data in Redux with manual loading flags, a single root Error Boundary, all form state in Redux, any on API response types, and 2 snapshot tests. Audit it and produce a complete blueprint for a new usage-based pricing feature.
steps: Step 1 — Audit: classify each finding by lens, evidence, impact, and priority. Finding 1 — Server state in Redux (Lens 4: State topology). Evidence: billingSlice.ts — manual loading/error/stale flags, no background refetch. Impact: users see stale invoices; manual invalidation logic is a recurring bug source. Priority: High. Effort: 3 days. Finding 2 — Single root Error Boundary (Lens 2: Async boundary). Evidence: App.tsx — one ErrorBoundary wrapping all routes. Impact: any render error in billing crashes the entire application. Priority: High. Effort: 0.5 days. Finding 3 — Form state in Redux (Lens 4: State topology). Evidence: paymentFormSlice.ts — field values, validation, submission state in store. Impact: 340 lines replaceable with RHF; form bugs from manual validation. Priority: Medium. Effort: 2 days. Finding 4 — any on API response types (Lens 3: TypeScript contracts). Evidence: billingApi.ts — fetchInvoices(): Promise<any>. Impact: type errors in invoice data reach production silently. Priority: Medium. Effort: 1 day. Finding 5 — No behavioral tests (Lens 6: Performance/testing). Evidence: 2 snapshot tests only. Impact: refactors break silently; billing regressions reach production. Priority: High. Effort: 3 days.

Step 2 — Prioritised remediation plan. Fix in order: (1) Error Boundaries first (0.5 days — immediate safety, prevents full-page crashes). (2) RTL + MSW tests (3 days — safety net established before further changes). (3) TanStack Query migration (3 days — eliminates the stale data bug class; now covered by tests). (4) RHF + Zod migration (2 days — best done after tests). (5) API type schemas (1 day — incremental). Rationale: Error Boundaries are cheapest and prevent catastrophic failures. Tests before refactoring establishes the safety net. State management changes while tests cover the behavior.

Step 3 — Blueprint state topology for usage-based pricing feature. Usage + plan data: Server cache, TanStack Query (staleTime: 60s). Invoice history: Server cache, TanStack Query (paginated). Upgrade modal open: Ephemeral, useState (local to parent). Upgrade form: Form, React Hook Form + Zod. Selected plan: Form field (within RHF state). Usage percentage: Derived, useMemo from usage/plan data. Key decisions: usage percentage is derived — never stored separately; upgrade modal is local — not a global store concern.

Step 4 — Blueprint async boundary map. <FeatureErrorBoundary> wraps the whole feature. UsagePanel: independent Suspense boundary — it loads from server. InvoiceHistory: nested FeatureErrorBoundary + Suspense — independent failure, independent loading. UpgradeModal: rendered as a Portal to escape stacking context; scoped FeatureErrorBoundary catches render errors. Each region fails or loads without affecting the others.

Step 5 — Blueprint TypeScript contracts and testing strategy. Contracts: PlanId and InvoiceId as branded types. upgradeSchema as Zod schema + z.infer type. Discriminated union for UsageState if not using Suspense. Testing: integration test for usage panel load (happy path); integration test for upgrade form submit + cache invalidation; integration test for error boundary activation on API failure; unit test for usage percentage calculation (pure useMemo function); E2E for full upgrade flow (revenue-critical).
workedExamples.conclusion: The audit and blueprint together represent the complete consulting deliverable: a prioritised diagnosis of what's wrong, an ordered remediation plan with cost estimates, and a forward-looking blueprint for new features that doesn't repeat the same mistakes. The ordering principle throughout: state topology first, async boundaries second, tests before large refactors, simplest fixes first.

retrievalPractice.prompt: From memory: name the six audit lenses and state the primary question each asks. For two lenses, give a concrete example of a finding that is often incorrectly assigned to the wrong lens.

Apply the liability documentation format to this finding: 'A billing module stores all server-fetched invoice data in a Redux slice. Users report seeing deleted invoices until they hard-refresh.' Fill in all six fields: LIABILITY, Lens, Evidence, Impact, Remediation, Priority.
What are the six sections of a complete architectural blueprint? Why must state topology come first, and what happens architecturally if component design comes first instead?

Translate this architectural decision into business language using the three-part framing: 'We are using Zod schemas as the single source of truth for both TypeScript types and runtime validation, replacing separately maintained interface definitions.'

Write a complete architectural recommendation for this scenario: Proposal A is headless hooks with Radix UI for an accessible component library. Proposal B is a CSS-in-JS monolithic component library. The team has 4 engineers, the product is a B2B dashboard, and accessibility is a contractual requirement. Apply all four recommendation parts: winning proposal, primary criterion, trade-off, review trigger.

SYNTHESIS — integrating all course competencies: A greenfield SaaS product brief describes: authenticated users, a dashboard with 5 data widgets (each from different API endpoints), a multi-step onboarding wizard, an admin panel (role-gated), and a public marketing page. Produce the full architectural blueprint in outline form: state topology table, async boundary map (describe it in text), library stack with justifications, and the first three integration tests you would write. This is the terminal synthesis exercise — draw on all eleven sections.

transferExercises.context: Legal case management system (law firm migrating from desktop to React)
transferExercises.scenario: A law firm's legacy desktop application has: all case data fetched on app load and stored in a single global object (no server cache layer), every component re-rendering whenever any case data changes, no TypeScript (plain JavaScript), form state for case notes managed in a global Redux store, and no tests. The firm's most urgent pain point: 'Lawyers sometimes see case data updated by a colleague but stale in their browser.'
transferExercises.task:
1. Apply all six audit lenses. Produce at least one finding per lens with evidence, impact, and remediation.
2. Prioritise the findings. Which liability is directly responsible for the urgent stale data pain point, and why does it rank highest?
3. The managing partner asks: 'Our developers say we need to refactor the state management — can you explain what that means and why it matters for us?' Write a 4-sentence business-language response.
4. Propose a phased remediation plan over three sprints for a 3-person team. Which liabilities do you address in each sprint, and why in that order?
successCriteria: Six lenses produce: (1) Rendering: global object update triggers full re-render cascade — fix: granular state ownership; (2) Async boundary: no Error Boundaries — any render error crashes full app; (3) TypeScript: no types — bugs invisible until runtime, refactoring unsafe; (4) State topology: server data in a global object with no staleness model — the stale data pain point; (5) Component design: monolithic components mixing fetch/logic/render; (6) Performance/testing: no tests, no code splitting.

State topology liability (server data in global object, no staleness model) ranks highest because it is directly causing the urgent user-facing pain point — lawyers seeing stale case data. Priority: High. Estimated impact: reduces stale data incidents to near zero.

Business translation: 'The state management change means the app will automatically check for updates in the background, so lawyers always see the current version of a case without needing to refresh. Right now, when a colleague updates a case, the next lawyer to open it may see old information — which creates risk in a legal context. The fix eliminates that risk structurally, rather than relying on each lawyer remembering to refresh. It also removes roughly 150 lines of manual data management code that our team currently maintains.'

Sprint plan: Sprint 1 — Error Boundaries (0.5 days, immediate safety) + TypeScript migration for the most critical modules (2-3 days, enables safe refactoring). Sprint 2 — TanStack Query for case data (3 days, directly fixes the stale data pain point) + basic RTL + MSW integration tests for the case list and case detail (2 days, safety net before further changes). Sprint 3 — Form state migration to RHF + Zod (2 days), component design improvements for the highest-traffic components (2 days). Rationale: safety first (Error Boundaries), then the pain point (server cache), then safe refactoring with a test net, then lower-priority improvements.

learningExperiences.title: Full Blueprint from a Product Brief: Practical Activity

contentBlocks.content:
PRACTICAL ACTIVITY: FULL BLUEPRINT FROM A PRODUCT BRIEF

Brief: 'We're building a Team Workload feature for our project management SaaS. It shows: a grid of team members with their assigned tasks and current capacity (server data, updates when tasks are reassigned), a drag-and-drop interface to reassign tasks between team members, a filter bar (by project, by date range), an overloaded warning banner that appears when any team member exceeds 100% capacity, and a settings panel where managers can set each member's maximum weekly hours.'

Produce a complete architectural blueprint:
1. State topology map — classify every implied state.
2. Async boundary map — draw the Suspense/Error Boundary placement.
3. Component model — identify which components are presentational, which are containers, which patterns apply.
4. TypeScript contracts — identify discriminated unions, branded types, and generic hooks needed.
5. Library selections — with explicit justification for each choice.
6. Testing strategy — three integration tests you would write first.

interactionSequence.prompt: For the Team Workload blueprint: the 'overloaded warning banner' appears when any team member exceeds 100% capacity. A developer proposes storing isOverloaded: boolean as useState. Apply the Section 5 state classification: what kind of state is isOverloaded? Should it be stored at all? If not, what is the correct mechanism — and what are the consequences if it's stored and the capacity data updates but the stored boolean doesn't?

feedback.correctiveFeedback: isOverloaded is derived state — computed from members data. Storing it creates a synchronisation hazard: capacity data updates via TanStack Query but the stored boolean lags by one render. Fix: useMemo from the members array, zero storage, zero synchronisation risk.
hints: Can isOverloaded be computed from data that already exists in the state topology?
What happens to the stored boolean when TanStack Query invalidates and refetches the members data?

confidencePrompt.prompt: How clearly did you identify the derived state classification and the synchronisation consequence?

learningExperiences.title: Capstone: Full Audit + Blueprint + Stakeholder Brief

selfGuidedExercise.realWorldContext: A real React codebase — a client project, an open-source application with meaningful complexity, or a significant personal project. The codebase should have at least one area where architectural improvement would be valuable.
selfGuidedExercise.objective: Produce the complete consulting deliverable for a real codebase: a structured audit with prioritised findings, a complete architectural blueprint for a new or rebuilt feature, and a one-page stakeholder communication brief. This is the professional artifact you would deliver at the end of an SMB SaaS engagement.
instructions: PART 1 — AUDIT (2 hours): Run all six lenses against the codebase. For each lens, apply at least one question and record findings in the liability documentation format. Aim for at least 5 findings total. Prioritise them as High, Medium, or Low with justification. Identify the single liability that, if fixed, would deliver the most value in the next 90 days.

PART 2 — BLUEPRINT (2 hours): Choose one significant feature that hasn't been built yet (or one that should be rebuilt). Produce a complete blueprint in the six-section format: (1) state topology map with classification and mechanism for every state piece, (2) async boundary map drawn as a JSX tree outline, (3) component model identifying presentational vs. containers and applicable patterns, (4) TypeScript contracts listing discriminated unions, branded types, and Zod schemas, (5) library selections with per-feature justification, (6) testing strategy naming three integration tests you would write first.

PART 3 — STAKEHOLDER BRIEF (1 hour): Take the top 3 findings from your audit. Write a one-page summary addressed to a non-technical product owner. For each finding: (a) what it is in one plain sentence, (b) what it costs the business in concrete terms, (c) what fixing it provides in concrete terms. No implementation jargon. The brief should be readable by someone with no React knowledge.
materialsOrPrerequisites: A React codebase with meaningful complexity (> 20 components, at least one data-fetching pattern, some form of state management)
Access to read the codebase, not necessarily to run it (though running it with DevTools helps)
reflectionPrompts: Which of the six audit lenses produced the most findings in your codebase? What does the distribution of findings across lenses tell you about the codebase's primary architectural weakness?
In the blueprint, did you find yourself wanting to start with the component design before the state topology? What stopped you, and what did you discover when you did state topology first?
In the stakeholder brief, which finding was hardest to translate into business language? What technical concept required the most rephrasing, and how did you ultimately frame it?

If a developer read your blueprint and had to implement the feature without asking you questions, could they? What would be the first question they would ask, and what does that tell you about the blueprint's completeness?

selfAssessmentCriteria: Audit: at least 5 findings documented with all six liability fields, prioritised, with the top-value finding identified
Blueprint: all six sections present, state topology first, specific enough for a developer to implement from
Stakeholder brief: three findings in plain language with concrete business costs and benefits, readable without React knowledge
Review triggers: at least one architectural decision in the blueprint includes an explicit review trigger
